text: The interlocking units that failed the EMC test must be reworked.
The interlocking units, which failed the EMC test, must be reworked.
The report issued on 14 July does not cover the grounding configuration.
Please confirm the standard to which the design was certified.
Thank you for joining, Ingrid. I want to be precise, so let me start with the scope. The six interlocking units that failed the EMC test on 14 July are the ones we are asking you to rework.
Good. That is what our response says. Six units, and the rework is at our cost. I am glad we agree.

We agree on the six. We do not agree on why. Those six units were built to the grounding configuration, which is common to all forty. So the question I am raising is not about six units. It is about a design.

Thirty-four units passed, João. If the configuration were the cause, all forty would have failed. The other six sit next to the traction return cable. It is an installation issue, and installation is your contractor.

That is a reasonable hypothesis, and it may well be right. But it is a hypothesis. Until somebody pins down the source of the interference, nobody can say whether it is the configuration or the position, and thirty-four units passing a test does not prove a design.
Then let us be practical. My team has devised a workaround: ferrite filters on the six units. They can be fitted in a week and the line runs.

I will take the filters for the trial run. I will not take them for the handover, because a workaround does not meet the acceptance criteria that apply at handover, and those criteria are in clause 7.4. What I need in writing is the standard to which the design was certified, and the conditions under which you tested it.

You have written this very carefully, haven't you. All right. You will have the certification standard and the test conditions in ten days. And I will run the emission scan on four of the units that passed, because if you are right, I would rather find it myself.
The six interlocking units that failed the EMC test must be reworked at the supplier's cost.
The grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, must be re-verified.
The workaround that your team devised does not meet the acceptance criteria that apply at handover.
Until we pin down the source of the interference, any fix is a guess.

João, it is Ingrid. Your ten days are up tomorrow, and I would rather you heard this from me than read it in a report. I ran the emission scan on four of the units that passed, as I said I would. Two of them are emitting just under the limit. They passed, but they passed by almost nothing, and a unit that passes by almost nothing is a unit that will fail on a bad day. So the design is marginal across the whole batch, and the six that failed were simply the ones in the worst position. You were right, and I was arguing a position rather than a finding. The grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, was verified on the bench and it was never verified in the installed condition. That is our gap, not yours, and it will be re-verified for all forty at our cost. You will have the certification standard and the full test conditions tomorrow morning, in writing, as you asked. Now the part I have to ask, and you are free to say no. Please keep the trial run on the ferrite filters, so that the line is not stopped while we do this properly. And one more thing. The sentence you wrote in that RFI is the reason I went back and looked. I could not read it two ways, so I had to go and find out which way it was.

Before you submit that RFI, three things, because engineers write these badly more often than anybody else. First, the comma. In a technical document the comma is not punctuation, it is scope, and scope is money. Write the units that failed the test, with no commas, and you have restricted it: those units, six of them, and nobody else. Write the units, comma, which failed the test, comma, and you have just told a German engineering team that all forty of their units failed, which is not true, and which is the first thing their lawyer will point out to you. It is the cheapest mistake in the language to make and the most expensive one to argue about afterwards. Second, and this is the one that wastes a month. Never put the word problem in a request for information. A problem is not a measurement. A problem invites an opinion, and you will get six opinions and no answer. State what was found, against which criterion, on which date, and then ask a question that can only be answered one way. A finding invites a response. Third, and this is the one that costs thirty years. Accept a workaround only with the date it dies. Write the filters in, if you must, but write in the day they come out, and write in what has to be in place before they come out. A temporary fix with no end date is not temporary. It quietly becomes the design, it goes into the as-built drawings, and the operator pays to maintain it long after everybody who agreed to it has left the project.
The six interlocking units that failed the EMC test must be reworked at the supplier's cost.
The grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, must be re-verified.
The report issued on 14 July does not cover the grounding configuration.
The units radiating above the limit are the six on the northbound platform.
Please confirm the standard to which the design was certified.
Please spell out the conditions under which the EMC test was performed.
The workaround that your team devised does not meet the acceptance criteria that apply at handover.
Until we pin down the source of the interference, any fix is a guess.
